guest: David Shulman, founder of Landor (brand naming company)
disagreements: Sam initially skeptical that naming matters; David convinced him via case studies (Swiffer $5B vs Ready Mop $200M)
dynamics: David teaches throughout with historical examples and naming frameworks. Sam and Shaan listen, ask probing questions, become convinced of naming's importance. David uses analogies (Ship of Gold treasure hunt) to explain creative process.
frameworks: Three things names must do: get attention, hold attention (processing fluency), be surprising
Right name vs good name vs okay name (right creates asymmetric advantage)
Treasure hunt methodology: generate 2000 names (trash → quality)
Two-person teams better than large brainstorming groups
Sound symbolism: P/K/B letters signal speed/reliability; CVCV pattern (Sonos) natural
Processing fluency: familiar yet surprising (surprisingly familiar)
The comfort trap: safe → invisible vs polarizing → energetic
Proof of concept: test names in context (headlines, ads) before deciding
learnings: Nothing in brand used more frequently or longer than the name
Name compounds in value over time; asymmetric advantage grows
Swiffer beat Ready Mop largely due to naming ($5B vs $200M brand value)
Quantity leads to quality: generate 2000 names, not 50
Brainstorming groups suffer from peer pressure and slow iteration
Small teams and individuals generate best names (not freelancers, not committees)
Creative permission critical: encourage problem-solving, avoid evaluation during ideation
Letters carry meaning: P/K/B convey speed; X conveys innovation; B is reliable
Polarizing names have energy; consensus names become invisible
Changing names not catastrophic if launched with story/enthusiasm
Databases matter: enrich periodic table, mythology, etc. with related knowledge
Synchronicity: connecting seemingly irrelevant ideas yields creative breakthroughs

banger_quotes.quote: Nothing you do in brand gets used more often or longer than your name
Polarizing names have energy; comfort names become invisible
We're looking for the Ship of Gold, not every shipwreck
You have to give people permission to fail
A polarizing name is good because it has energy to it

numbers_dropped: Ready Mop (Clorox competitor): $200 million brand value
Landor project cost range: $75K-$150K typical, up to $200K+ for major corporate
Pentium sound symbolism testing: 6 countries, multiple languages
BlackBerry adoption rate: took multiple pitches and research to convince client
notable: David has named Pentium, Swiffer, Febreze, Sonos, BlackBerry, Impossible Burger, Microsoft Azure, and 50+ billion-dollar brands. He emphasizes that great names compound in value and that the difference between Swiffer and Ready Mop (same product category, different names) is roughly $5B - demonstrating name's ROI. Episode ends with Sam confessing he's been convinced that naming is critical to success.
